title: Metals and Non-metals
summary: Chapter 3 covers the physical and chemical properties of metals and non-metals. Students learn about the reactivity series and how metals react with oxygen, water, acids, and other metal salts. The extraction of metals from ores involves steps like roasting, calcination, smelting, and refining. Corrosion of metals and prevention methods like galvanisation and alloying are also studied.
learningPoints: State physical properties of metals: lustre, hardness, malleability, ductility, conductivity
State physical properties of non-metals: brittle, non-lustrous, poor conductors
Write equations for metals reacting with oxygen, water, acids, and other metal salts
State the reactivity series of metals (K>Na>Ca>Mg>Al>Zn>Fe>Pb>H>Cu>Hg>Ag>Au)
Explain steps in metal extraction: crushing, roasting/calcination, smelting, refining
Define alloy and give examples: brass, bronze, amalgam, stainless steel
Define corrosion and explain rusting of iron
Explain prevention of corrosion: painting, galvanisation, alloying, anodisation

laws.name: Reactivity Series
laws.statement: K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Pb > H > Cu > Hg > Ag > Au > Pt. Metals above hydrogen displace H₂ from acids. More reactive metals displace less reactive metals from their salt solutions.

formulas.label: Roasting
formulas.description: Heating sulphide ore in excess air to convert to oxide.

formulas.label: Calcination
formulas.description: Heating carbonate ore in limited air to convert to oxide.

formulas.label: Smelting (Reduction)
formulas.description: Reducing metal oxide with coke (carbon) to get metal.

formulas.label: Thermite Reaction
formulas.description: Aluminium reduces iron oxide. Al is more reactive than Fe.

formulas.label: Rusting of Iron
formulas.description: Iron reacts with oxygen and water to form hydrated iron oxide (rust).

formulas.label: Electrolysis of Copper
formulas.expression: At anode: Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ | At cathode: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu
formulas.description: Impure copper anode dissolves; pure copper deposits at cathode.

diagrams.title: Electrolytic Refining of Copper
diagrams.description: Impure Cu anode, pure Cu cathode, CuSO₄ electrolyte. Impurities fall as anode mud.

diagrams.title: Reactivity Series
diagrams.description: K, Na, Ca, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe, Pb, H, Cu, Hg, Ag, Au listed from most to least reactive.

diagrams.title: Blast Furnace
diagrams.description: Iron is extracted from haematite (Fe₂O₃) in a blast furnace using coke and limestone.

resources.label: Karnataka SSLC Science Textbook
resources.description: Official Karnataka SSLC Science textbook. Chapter 3 covers metals and non-metals.

resources.label: NCERT Science Textbook Class 10
resources.description: NCERT Class 10 Science Chapter 3 Metals and Non-metals.

resources.label: Metals and Non-metals — Video Lessons
resources.description: Khan Academy videos on reactivity series, extraction and corrosion.

pyqs.question: The constituent metals in brass are: (A) copper and zinc (B) aluminium and tin (C) copper and tin (D) zinc and aluminium
pyqs.answer: (A) copper and zinc. Brass = Cu + Zn. Bronze = Cu + Sn.

pyqs.question: Explain the formation of sodium chloride molecule by transfer of electrons.
pyqs.answer: Na (2,8,1) loses 1 electron → Na⁺ (2,8). Cl (2,8,7) gains 1 electron → Cl⁻ (2,8,8). Na⁺ and Cl⁻ attract each other by electrostatic force → NaCl (ionic bond).

pyqs.question: What are the products obtained when aluminium metal reacts with steam?
pyqs.answer: Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃) and Hydrogen gas (H₂). 2Al + 3H₂O → Al₂O₃ + 3H₂

pyqs.question: What is roasting in metallurgy?
pyqs.answer: The process of converting sulphide ore into oxide by heating strongly in excess air at high temperature is called roasting. 2ZnS + 3O₂ → 2ZnO + 2SO₂

pyqs.question: Give reason: Magnesium metal displaces copper in copper sulphate solution.
pyqs.answer: Magnesium is more reactive than copper in the reactivity series. Mg + CuSO₄ → MgSO₄ + Cu.

pyqs.question: Give reason: Sodium metal is stored in kerosene.
pyqs.answer: Sodium is highly reactive and reacts rapidly with atmospheric oxygen and water. Kerosene does not react with sodium and protects it from air and moisture.

mcqs.question: The constituent metals in brass are:
mcqs.explanation: Brass = Cu + Zn. Bronze = Cu + Sn. Solder = Pb + Sn. Amalgam = Hg + another metal.

mcqs.question: To make iron hard and strong:
options: A. Carbon is added to pure iron
C. It is fused with some metals
mcqs.explanation: Steel is made by adding carbon to iron. 0.5–1.5% carbon makes it hard (steel). Stainless steel has Cr and Ni added.

mcqs.question: What is roasting in metallurgy?
options: A. Heating carbonate ore in limited air
B. Heating sulphide ore in excess air
mcqs.explanation: Roasting: 2ZnS + 3O₂ → 2ZnO + 2SO₂. Sulphide ore heated in excess air converts to oxide.

mcqs.question: Sodium is stored in kerosene because:
options: A. Kerosene reacts with Na to form NaOH
B. Na reacts rapidly with air and water
mcqs.explanation: Na is highly reactive — reacts with O₂ and H₂O violently. Kerosene is inert to Na and protects it.

mcqs.question: In electrolytic refining of copper, pure copper is deposited at:
mcqs.explanation: At cathode: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu. Impure copper at anode dissolves; pure copper deposits at cathode.

mcqs.question: The most reactive metal in the reactivity series is:
mcqs.explanation: Reactivity series from top: K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe. Potassium is most reactive.

mcqs.question: Galvanisation protects iron from rusting by coating it with:
mcqs.explanation: Galvanisation is coating iron with zinc. Even if zinc layer is scratched, zinc (being more reactive) oxidises first protecting iron.

mcqs.question: Products obtained when aluminium reacts with steam are:
mcqs.explanation: 2Al + 3H₂O → Al₂O₃ + 3H₂. Al reacts with steam but not cold water due to oxide layer.

mcqs.question: Which property allows metals to be drawn into wires?
mcqs.explanation: Ductility is the ability to be drawn into thin wires. Gold and silver are most ductile metals.

mcqs.question: Thermite reaction is: Fe₂O₃ + 2Al → Al₂O₃ + 2Fe. This shows:
options: A. Al is less reactive than Fe
B. Al is more reactive than Fe
mcqs.explanation: More reactive Al displaces less reactive Fe from Fe₂O₃. This confirms Al is above Fe in reactivity series.

mcqs.question: An ore of a metal is:
options: A. Any mineral containing the metal
B. Mineral from which metal can be profitably extracted
C. Pure form of the metal
mcqs.explanation: Ore = mineral from which metal can be extracted economically and profitably. Not all minerals are ores.

mcqs.question: Rusting of iron requires:
mcqs.explanation: 4Fe + 3O₂ + xH₂O → 2Fe₂O₃·xH₂O. Both O₂ and H₂O are essential for rusting.

mcqs.question: Calcination is heating carbonate ore in:
mcqs.explanation: Calcination: ZnCO₃ → ZnO + CO₂. Carbonate ore is heated in limited/absence of air to get metal oxide.

mcqs.question: How is pure zinc obtained from zinc oxide?
mcqs.explanation: ZnO + C → Zn + CO. Zinc oxide is reduced by coke (carbon) at high temperature.

mcqs.question: The anode mud in electrolytic refining of copper contains:
options: C. Impurities like gold and silver
mcqs.explanation: Impurities like Au, Ag, Pt from anode fall as anode mud. These precious metals are recovered.

exercises.question: Explain the formation of ionic bond in MgCl₂ with electron dot structure.
exercises.answer: Mg (2,8,2) loses 2 electrons → Mg²⁺. Two Cl atoms (2,8,7) each gain 1 electron → 2Cl⁻. Mg²⁺ and 2Cl⁻ attract each other by electrostatic force → MgCl₂. Electron dot: Mg → Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻; 2Cl + 2e⁻ → 2Cl⁻.

exercises.question: Draw a labelled diagram of electrolytic refining of copper.
exercises.answer: Setup: Impure copper (anode), pure copper (cathode), CuSO₄ solution (electrolyte). Process: At anode Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ (dissolves). At cathode Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (deposits). Impurities fall as anode mud.

exercises.question: List the steps in extraction of metal from its ore.
exercises.answer: (i) Crushing and grinding ore (ii) Concentration/enriching ore (iii) Roasting/calcination to get metal oxide (iv) Reduction of oxide to get metal (v) Refining (electrolytic or zone refining).

exercises.question: Explain why most metals are found as compounds in nature.
exercises.answer: Metals are reactive and react with O₂, CO₂, H₂O, S, etc. in nature to form stable compounds (oxides, carbonates, sulphides). Only very unreactive metals like Au, Pt, Ag are found in free state.

exercises.question: Give four methods to prevent rusting of iron.
exercises.answer: (i) Painting/oiling — barrier from O₂ and H₂O (ii) Galvanisation — coating with zinc (iii) Alloying — making stainless steel with Cr and Ni (iv) Electroplating — coating with chromium or nickel.

exercises.question: Distinguish between roasting and calcination.
exercises.answer: Roasting: heating sulphide ore in excess air (2ZnS + 3O₂ → 2ZnO + 2SO₂). Calcination: heating carbonate ore in limited air (ZnCO₃ → ZnO + CO₂). Both convert ore to metal oxide for reduction.

exercises.question: Name five alloys and their constituent metals.
exercises.answer: (i) Brass = Cu + Zn (ii) Bronze = Cu + Sn (iii) Stainless steel = Fe + Cr + Ni (iv) Solder = Pb + Sn (v) Amalgam = Hg + another metal. Alloys have better properties than pure metals.

exercises.question: Why is it more difficult to extract Na than Fe from their ores?
exercises.answer: Na is more reactive (higher in reactivity series) and forms very stable Na₂O. Carbon cannot reduce Na₂O. Na must be extracted by electrolysis of molten NaCl (Downs process). Fe is less reactive — extracted by reduction with coke.
